C:
Use Tailwind. For a new React app today it's the stronger default, and the main reason CSS-in-JS lost ground is technical, not just fashion.

## Recommendation: Tailwind

**The decisive reason: runtime CSS-in-JS conflicts with where React is going.** Runtime libraries (styled-components, Emotion) generate styles during render in the browser. That adds a runtime cost on every render, and — more importantly — it doesn't play well with React Server Components and streaming SSR (Next.js App Router, and increasingly the default for new apps). styled-components has effectively wound down active development, and the Emotion/RSC story is awkward. If you reach for CSS-in-JS, you'd want a *zero-runtime* variant (vanilla-extract, PandaCSS, StyleX) — which is a smaller, less-documented ecosystem than either Tailwind or classic CSS-in-JS. So the "CSS-in-JS" you're probably picturing is the version that's fading.

**Tailwind's concrete wins:**
- **No runtime.** Styles are extracted at build time to a static CSS file. Nothing ships a styling engine to the browser.
- **Works with anything** — Server Components, SSR, RSC, plain SPA. No provider, no SSR-hydration setup.
- **Design constraints come for free.** The spacing/color/type scale keeps a team consistent without you building a token system by hand.
- **Large talent pool and ecosystem.** It's the de facto default, so component libraries (shadcn/ui, Radix-based kits) and hiring both assume it.

**The honest costs:**
- Verbose, "ugly" className strings. Real, and the most common complaint. `@apply` and component extraction mitigate it but don't erase it.
- Truly dynamic, value-driven styles (e.g. a color computed from data at runtime) are awkward — that's where you drop to an inline `style` prop or a CSS variable.

## When I'd pick CSS-in-JS instead

- You need **heavily runtime-dynamic styling** — theming that's computed from data, design-tool-like products where styles are values, not classes.
- You're shipping a **component library** meant to drop into arbitrary host apps with scoped, collision-proof styles and a typed theme API — colocated styles are a genuine advantage there.

In those cases, choose a **zero-runtime** library (vanilla-extract or PandaCSS) rather than a render-time one, so you keep SSR/RSC compatibility.

## Bottom line

For a typical new React app — product UI, dashboard, marketing site, anything SSR or Next.js — **start with Tailwind.** It's the lowest-friction, best-supported, most future-proof choice, and the className verbosity is a cosmetic tradeoff you can manage. Only go CSS-in-JS if your app is genuinely styling-as-data or you're publishing a reusable component library, and even then prefer a zero-runtime option.

One practical note: this isn't all-or-nothing. Tailwind plus occasional plain CSS Modules for a few complex components is a common, healthy combination — you don't need a second styling paradigm to handle the 5% of cases Tailwind handles poorly.

- **Design constraints come for free.** The spacing/color/type scale keeps a team consistent without you building a token system by hand.

**The honest costs:**
- Verbose, "ugly" className strings. Real, and the most common complaint. `@apply` and component extraction mitigate it but don't erase it.
- Truly dynamic, value-driven styles (e.g. a color computed from data at runtime) are awkward — that's where you drop to an inline `style` prop or a CSS variable.

## When I'd pick CSS-in-JS instead

- You need **heavily runtime-dynamic styling** — theming that's computed from data, design-tool-like products where styles are values, not classes.
- You're shipping a **component library** meant to drop into arbitrary host apps with scoped, collision-proof styles and a typed theme API — colocated styles are a genuine advantage there.

In those cases, choose a **zero-runtime** library (vanilla-extract or PandaCSS) rather than a render-time one, so you keep SSR/RSC compatibility.

## Bottom line

For a typical new React app — product UI, dashboard, marketing site, anything SSR or Next.js — **start with Tailwind.** It's the lowest-friction, best-supported, most future-proof choice, and the className verbosity is a cosmetic tradeoff you can manage. Only go CSS-in-JS if your app is genuinely styling-as-data or you're publishing a reusable component library, and even then prefer a zero-runtime option.

**What would change this recommendation.** The case for Tailwind rests on one assumption: that your app's styling is mostly static and class-expressible. If most of your UI is genuinely styling-as-data — values computed at runtime, a theming engine, a design-tool-like canvas — that assumption breaks and a zero-runtime CSS-in-JS library becomes the better fit. It would also shift if your team has deep existing CSS-in-JS expertise and tooling and the app is a client-only SPA with no RSC/SSR plans, since the biggest technical knock against runtime CSS-in-JS (the RSC/streaming-SSR friction) simply doesn't apply there. The key limitation of this advice: it's a default for the common case, not a verdict for every codebase — the more your styling depends on runtime-computed values, the weaker it gets.

One practical note: this isn't all-or-nothing. Tailwind plus occasional plain CSS Modules for a few complex components is a common, healthy combination — you don't need a second styling paradigm to handle the 5% of cases Tailwind handles poorly.
P:
**Use Tailwind for a new React app.** It's the stronger default today, and the reason CSS-in-JS has lost ground is technical, not just fashion — so for most apps this isn't a close call.

## Why Tailwind wins

**The decisive reason: runtime CSS-in-JS is fighting where React is going.** Runtime libraries (styled-components, Emotion) generate styles in the browser during render. That costs something on every render, and — more importantly — it fits poorly with React Server Components and streaming SSR, which the Next.js App Router has made the default path for new apps. styled-components went into maintenance mode in 2025, and Emotion's RSC story is still awkward. If you wanted CSS-in-JS today, you'd reach for a *zero-runtime* variant (vanilla-extract, Panda CSS, StyleX) — a smaller, less-documented ecosystem than either Tailwind or classic CSS-in-JS. In other words, the CSS-in-JS most people picture is the version that's fading.

Tailwind's concrete advantages:
- **No runtime.** Styles are extracted at build time to a static CSS file; nothing ships a styling engine to the browser. (Tailwind v4's rewritten engine made that build step substantially faster.)
- **Works with everything** — Server Components, SSR, RSC, plain SPA — with no provider and no SSR-hydration wiring.
- **Design constraints for free.** The built-in spacing/color/type scale keeps a team consistent without hand-rolling a token system.
- **Default-sized ecosystem and talent pool.** Component kits (shadcn/ui, Radix-based libraries) and most hiring already assume it.

The honest costs:
- **Verbose className strings** — the most common complaint, and a real one. Component extraction and `@apply` reduce it but don't erase it.
- **Truly dynamic, value-driven styles** — a color computed from data at runtime — are awkward; that's where you drop to an inline `style` prop or a CSS variable.

## When CSS-in-JS is the better call

- You need **heavily runtime-dynamic styling**: theming computed from data, or design-tool-like products where styles *are* values rather than fixed classes.
- You're shipping a **reusable component library** meant to drop into arbitrary host apps, where scoped, collision-proof styles and a typed theme API genuinely earn their keep.

Even then, choose a **zero-runtime** library (vanilla-extract or Panda CSS) over a render-time one, so you keep SSR/RSC compatibility.

## Bottom line

For a typical new React app — product UI, dashboard, marketing site, anything on Next.js — **start with Tailwind.** It's the lowest-friction, best-supported, most future-proof choice, and the className verbosity is a cosmetic tradeoff you can manage. Go CSS-in-JS only if your app is genuinely styling-as-data or you're publishing a component library — and prefer a zero-runtime option there too.

And it isn't all-or-nothing: Tailwind plus the occasional plain CSS Module for a few genuinely complex components is a common, healthy setup. You don't need a second styling paradigm to cover the 5% of cases Tailwind handles poorly.
